items.stem: Yields fall by a full 2 percentage points. For an ordinary, option-free bond, how does the actual price change compare with the estimate from modified duration?

options.text: The actual gain is larger than duration predicted
options.explanation: Correct. The price–yield curve bows toward the holder, so on a big downward move in yields, positive convexity adds to the straight-line estimate. Duration undershoots the gain.

options.text: The actual gain is smaller than duration predicted
options.explanation: That's the signature of NEGATIVE convexity — prepayable or callable bonds. A plain bond's curvature works for you, not against you.

options.text: Exactly what duration predicted — that's what duration is for
options.explanation: Duration is exact only in the limit of tiny moves. Two full percentage points is far outside straight-line territory; the curvature takes over.

options.text: The price falls — large moves reverse the see-saw
options.explanation: The see-saw never reverses: yields down, price up. Convexity changes the SIZE of the move, never its direction.

items.stem: Rates fall sharply, but a callable bond's price rises only slightly and then stalls, well short of comparable non-callable bonds. Why?

options.text: The market expects the issuer to redeem the bond early at the call price, which caps the upside
options.explanation: Exactly. Falling rates make refinancing attractive to the ISSUER — and the issuer holds the option. The price can't rise far past the call price once redemption looks likely. Capped gains on a rate rally is negative convexity in action.
They have duration — it's their CONVEXITY that's pathological. The price still responds to rates; it just bends the wrong way as the call comes into view.

options.text: The issuer's credit must have deteriorated at the same time
options.explanation: No credit event is needed — the stall is a pure structure effect. The embedded option explains it on its own.

options.text: Falling rates hurt all corporate bonds
options.explanation: Falling rates lift ordinary bond prices — that's the comparison in the question. Only the embedded call explains why this one lags.
